title: Typed parameter $X clobbered by reassignment to differently-cased local $x
description: PowerShell variables are case-insensitive: `$Top` and `$top` are the same variable. If a function declares `[int]$Top = 25` then later writes `$top = @($rows)`, the type constraint from the parameter declaration still applies, and PowerShell raises 'Cannot convert System.Object[] to System.Int32' at the assignment site (or at first use of `$Top` in pipeline binding). Detected when Get-SinHeatmap was called with `-Top 20` and threw the conversion error from inside the SVG-render branch where a local `$top = @($rows)` shadowed the parameter.
fix_strategy: Rename the local to something disjoint (e.g. `$rowsArr` instead of `$top`).
Treat function parameters as read-only; never reassign them.
If reassignment is required, drop the type constraint or use Set-Variable -Option None first.
examples_bad: function Foo { param([int]$Top = 25); $top = @($rows) } # throws 'Cannot convert Object[] to Int32'

first_seen.outcome: ERR: Cannot convert the System.Object[] value of type System.Object[] to type System.Int32 (line 37)
